description: CRM platform with customer data, sales automation, and extensible APIs for platform and business objects.
auth_notes: Official Salesforce docs show OAuth 2.0 for connected apps and list OAuth authorization flows including username-password, client credentials, and JWT bearer flows. Core API calls also use Salesforce session/access tokens after auth. No first-party evidence was verified for API key auth for core Salesforce APIs from the gathered pages, so API key is not included. Username-password flow is classified under other because allowed enum does not include it directly.
gating_reason: API access depends on Salesforce edition. Gathered official limits/access evidence indicates Enterprise, Unlimited, and Performance include API access, while Professional requires an add-on/activation and some lower tiers may not include API access by default.

api_surface.breadth: Broad public API surface including REST APIs and GraphQL API, with platform/business object access plus Connect/Chatter-related APIs and documented limits pages.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Salesforce has official MCP support evidence from Salesforce Developers, including the Salesforce DX MCP Server repository under the Salesforce CLI GitHub org and official hosted MCP server documentation/blog material.

blocker: Public docs and auth exist, but first toolkit build may require a paid Salesforce edition with API access enabled; edition-dependent entitlement creates a practical access workaround rather than fully self-serve readiness.
rate_limit_notes: Official limits docs indicate 24-hour API request allocations by edition/license, concurrent long-running request limits (25 for production/sandbox orgs and 5 for developer/trial orgs in gathered evidence), and GraphQL uses Connect REST API limits; Connect REST/Chatter also has per-user, per-application, per-hour limits.
notable_signals: Official REST and GraphQL docs are public; Salesforce has first-party MCP momentum with Salesforce DX MCP Server and broader Salesforce MCP announcements; API entitlement is edition-sensitive rather than universally self-serve.
human_review_reason: Some conclusions rely on extracted summaries from browser runs because several Salesforce docs/help pages rendered inconsistently. API-key non-support for core APIs was inferred from absence in gathered first-party auth pages rather than a directly quoted denial, and exact edition/pricing entitlement details may vary by product/plan.

description: CRM platform with APIs for customer data, marketing, sales, service, and CMS workflows.
auth_notes: Official HubSpot docs support OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow for public apps and Bearer access tokens for private apps; the available evidence also indicates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE for HubSpot's remote MCP server. Private app documentation URL could not be directly opened in-browser due to login redirect, so token-auth details rely on gathered official-search evidence and should be human-verified.
gating_reason: Public developer docs are available, HubSpot offers self-serve developer access, and API usage is available without partner approval; limits vary by subscription tier and add-ons.

api_surface.breadth: Broad API surface across CRM objects, marketing, sales, service, CMS, rate-limit/account endpoints, and an official GraphQL API in addition to REST APIs.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Evidence gathered indicates HubSpot provides official MCP offerings: a remote HubSpot MCP server at mcp.hubspot.com and a local developer MCP flow via HubSpot CLI (`hs mcp setup`).

rate_limit_notes: Gathered official evidence indicates HubSpot enforces subscription- and app-type-based limits. Public OAuth apps are limited to 110 requests per 10 seconds. Private apps have burst and daily limits that vary by tier, with published API-limit pricing/add-on details and 429 responses on overage.
notable_signals: Official GraphQL support is documented alongside REST APIs. HubSpot appears to have official MCP support, which is a strong positive signal for agent tooling. API-limit increases are productized on a pricing page rather than being partner-gated.
human_review_reason: Some details, especially private-app token auth specifics and exact self-serve/free availability nuances, were inferred from gathered official-search evidence rather than directly opened source pages, and the private-app page encountered a login redirect during browser research.

description: CRM platform for managing sales pipelines, contacts, deals, and related business workflows.
auth_notes: Official docs describe OAuth 2.0 for marketplace/public apps and API token auth via the x-api-token header for direct API access.
gating_reason: Pipedrive Developer Hub offers self-serve app registration and a sandbox account from the public developers site; no partner approval was verified as required for initial API access.

api_surface.breadth: Public API documentation covers Core API concepts and versioned REST endpoints (v1 and v2) for CRM objects and related operations; no official GraphQL surface was found in the reviewed first-party docs.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Pipedrive MCP server page or repository was found in the reviewed first-party sources; absence is not definitive for community MCPs.

rate_limit_notes: Official rate-limit docs describe a token-based daily budget: 30,000 base tokens multiplied by plan multiplier and seat count, with rollout starting for new customers in Dec 2024 and for existing customers during 2025.
notable_signals: Public developer portal, sandbox availability, versioned API docs, and documented OAuth plus API-token auth indicate strong near-term toolkit buildability. Rate limiting is clearly documented and tied to account plan/seats.
human_review_reason: MCP availability could not be definitively ruled out beyond reviewed first-party sources, and self-serve access details were inferred from the public developer/sandbox pages rather than a dedicated pricing or access-policy page.

description: Data-driven CRM for modern teams with customizable objects, syncing, and automation.
auth_notes: Attio documents OAuth 2.0 for third-party integrations and API keys for internal use; API keys can be sent as Bearer tokens or via HTTP Basic auth. The hosted MCP server uses OAuth.
gating_reason: Attio offers a self-serve Free plan with 'Start for free' signup and public API documentation; higher tiers add capabilities like SQL/SCIM but basic platform access is not gated.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API with broad CRM coverage across objects, attributes, records, lists, entries, workspace members, notes, tasks, threads, comments, meetings, call recordings, files, webhooks, plus documented SQL and SCIM surfaces for higher tiers.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Attio publishes an official hosted MCP server at mcp.attio.com with first-party docs describing OAuth connection and AI client availability.

rate_limit_notes: REST API docs state 100 read requests/sec and 25 write requests/sec per workspace. Lists/list entries also use score-based limits (typically 1,000 points/minute). MCP docs note additional limits such as search at 300/minute and semantic search/reporting at 2/sec.
notable_signals: Official first-party hosted MCP server; public developer docs; self-serve free tier; API docs emphasize OAuth and agent connectivity. Enterprise-tier signals include SCIM/SSO and SQL access.

description: Open-source CRM for technical teams with customizable data model and workflow building blocks.
auth_notes: Twenty documents Personal Access Tokens used as Bearer tokens for individual/server access, and OAuth for external applications accessing workspace data.
gating_reason: Public pricing page indicates API access and MCP server are included on paid Pro and Organization plans; advanced developer-related limits/features scale by plan.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer docs state both REST and GraphQL APIs are available for extending Twenty and accessing workspace data.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Twenty publicly lists an MCP server as a Developers feature on its pricing page, indicating first-party MCP support, but the exact repository/docs page for the MCP server was not verified in available evidence.

blocker: API appears buildable from public docs, but self-serve free access to hosted API was not verified; pricing evidence points to paid plans for API access/MCP, so initial toolkit development likely requires a paid plan or self-hosting confirmation.
rate_limit_notes: Developer docs mention a default limit of 100 requests per minute for API keys; pricing page shows plan-based API call limits of 50/min for Pro and 100/min for Organization, creating some ambiguity that should be checked during implementation.
notable_signals: Official public developer documentation exists; API supports both REST and GraphQL; auth supports PAT/Bearer and OAuth; pricing page explicitly references an MCP server and developer feature set; Twenty is positioned as open-source/customizable CRM.
human_review_reason: Pricing vs docs create ambiguity on exact API availability and rate limits across free/self-hosted vs paid cloud plans, and MCP existence is only confirmed from pricing language rather than a directly verified MCP documentation or repository page.

description: CRM platform for deal, relationship, and pipeline management with a documented DealCloud API for customer data and workflow integrations.
auth_notes: Official docs include an API Keys guide and a token-based auth guide; evidence gathered indicates API key generation requires an existing DealCloud account plus specific user-group/API capability permissions. The exact token standard and whether OAuth2 is formally supported were not fully verified from final extracted text, so token auth is marked with human review.
gating_reason: API access appears customer/admin gated rather than public self-serve: gathered evidence indicates users need an existing DealCloud account and permission to generate API keys via an API capability/user-group setting.

api_surface.breadth: Public docs show a broad documented API with guides, API references, limits, SDK sections, release notes, and product areas including authentication, user management, data, schema, RelIntelligence, publications, and eMarketing.
api_surface.mcp_notes: The official docs include an /mcp section and gathered evidence noted an MCP server/client preview, but implementation maturity and public availability details remain unclear.

blocker: Public documentation exists, but initial toolkit build likely requires being an existing DealCloud customer and obtaining admin-enabled API access/credentials; some auth and MCP details remain ambiguous from the gathered evidence.
rate_limit_notes: Browser-gathered evidence from the official limits page indicates documented per-area limits, with notes mentioning values such as 100 requests/minute, 2,000 requests/hour, and 10,000 requests/day; exact endpoint-by-endpoint mapping should be rechecked manually on the limits table.
notable_signals: Official API docs are publicly accessible; docs include SDK pages (including C# and Python sections), release notes/changelog, explicit limits documentation, and an MCP page, all of which are positive signals for agent/toolkit feasibility.
human_review_reason: Need manual verification of the exact token auth type (token vs OAuth2), the precise rate-limit table values by API area, whether MCP is publicly usable today vs preview, and whether access tier should be classified as paid-plan-required versus admin-approval-required based on customer entitlement details.

description: Customer service and helpdesk platform with ticketing, help center, and support APIs.
auth_notes: Official Zendesk API docs indicate OAuth access tokens and API tokens; API-token authentication is performed via Basic authentication. It is unclear from gathered evidence whether password-based basic auth remains broadly supported vs legacy/deprecated for all products, so this is marked for review.
gating_reason: Official pricing evidence shows API access as a plan feature on paid Zendesk plans such as Support Team, Suite Team, Suite Professional, and Suite Enterprise; no verified free self-serve API tier was gathered.

api_surface.breadth: Broad public API surface across Zendesk support/ticketing resources. Official docs gathered confirm a JSON-based REST API for the main Zendesk API, and browser evidence also found an official GraphQL query API for a Zendesk Sell/Sales CRM area, so surface appears mixed across product lines rather than GraphQL-first.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Browser evidence found third-party Zendesk MCP server repositories on GitHub, but no official Zendesk MCP server or official MCP registry entry was verified. Treat as community MCP only.

blocker: API access appears tied to paid plans, and some details (official MCP status and exact auth scope across products) remain ambiguous from gathered evidence.
rate_limit_notes: Official rate-limits documentation indicates plan-based limits for Support and Help Center APIs, with browser-extracted evidence showing Zendesk Suite limits ranging roughly from 200 to 2500 requests per minute depending on plan, plus endpoint-specific limits and rate-limit headers documented on the rate-limits page.
notable_signals: Official docs clearly expose authentication, request format, and rate-limit guidance. Pricing comparison shows API access as a visible developer-tools feature on multiple paid plans, and additional API volume appears available as an add-on for some larger plans. No official Zendesk MCP evidence was found; community GitHub MCP repos do exist.
human_review_reason: Uncertainty remains on whether to classify auth as only oauth2+api_key versus also basic because API-token auth uses Basic headers; GraphQL support appears limited to a specific Zendesk product area rather than the main Support API; and no official MCP server was verified, only third-party repositories.

description: CRM and sales engagement platform with API access for core CRM, communications, and automation workflows.
auth_notes: Official docs support OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens and API keys sent via HTTP Basic auth where the API key is the username and password is empty; official MCP docs also indicate API-key auth with MCP scopes.
gating_reason: Close pricing and FAQ indicate self-serve signup and trial without talking to sales.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API at https://api.close.com/api/v1 covering CRM core objects, activities, events/webhooks, search/reporting, plus an official remote MCP server for agent access.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Close MCP server is documented at help.close.com/docs/mcp-server with endpoint https://mcp.close.com/mcp; a community MCP server also exists on GitHub.

rate_limit_notes: Official docs say rate limits are enforced per endpoint group, at the organization level across all users' API keys, with an additional lower per-API-key limit. Most responses include a ratelimit header, and 429 responses include ratelimit and retry-after headers.
notable_signals: Official docs explicitly state the API uses REST conventions; pricing indicates API access is included on standard plans and self-serve signup is available; official MCP support for Claude and ChatGPT is a strong agent-readiness signal.
human_review_reason: Pricing-task output contained conflicting detail about which plans include API access, and the docs evidence gathered was summarized by the browser rather than manually cross-checked line-by-line here; access-tier specifics and whether Solo includes API access should be confirmed by a human if plan granularity matters.

description: Customer relationship management platform for sales automation, customer data, and pipeline workflows.
auth_notes: Official Zoho CRM API v6 docs state the API uses OAuth 2.0; evidence gathered did not verify any other public auth method for CRM API access.
gating_reason: Official API limits page includes a Free Edition with 5,000 API credits, indicating self-serve API access is available without sales gating.

api_surface.breadth: Official developer docs show broad CRM API coverage including RESTful Entity APIs for CRUD operations and a documented GraphQL API surface in API v6.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official MCP Registry search showed community-contributed Zoho CRM servers, but no first-party Zoho MCP server was verified from the gathered evidence.

rate_limit_notes: Zoho CRM API uses a credit-based rolling 24-hour window by edition. Gathered evidence indicates Free Edition has 5,000 credits; higher editions scale credits by user count. Simultaneous call limits range from 5 to 25 depending on edition, with a fixed sub-concurrency limit of 10 for some heavy operations.
notable_signals: Official docs indicate both REST and GraphQL support, OAuth 2.0 with refresh tokens, and purchasable extra API credits via API Dashboard. Official MCP Registry contains community Zoho CRM MCP entries, suggesting ecosystem support even without verified first-party MCP.
human_review_reason: MCP availability is only verified via registry search results and appears community-maintained rather than first-party; also access-tier interpretation assumes Free Edition API availability from the limits page without independently confirming all signup prerequisites.

description: AI-powered helpdesk platform with a natively integrated AI agent for customer support.
auth_notes: Intercom documents OAuth for public apps and access tokens for private apps created within a workspace.
gating_reason: Developer docs and app creation are publicly available; evidence gathered indicates standard API access is self-serve, while some advanced capabilities such as Fin over API and extended API limits are plan- or sales-gated.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API with broad resources such as conversations, contacts, companies, articles, data attributes, plus webhooks, Canvas components, and an official remote MCP server.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Intercom documents an official remote MCP server and an official GitHub repository (intercom-mcp-server); docs note current availability is limited to US-hosted workspaces.

rate_limit_notes: Docs state 10,000 calls per minute per app for private/public apps and 25,000 calls per minute per workspace across all apps; rate-limit headers include X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset.
notable_signals: Official developer hub, explicit auth docs, rate-limit docs, broad REST reference, and official MCP documentation/GitHub repo are strong signals. MCP is limited to US-hosted workspaces; pricing evidence also suggests some advanced API-related features are gated separately from baseline API access.
human_review_reason: The gathered pricing evidence suggests baseline API access is self-serve, but the exact plan-level availability of all standard API capabilities versus advanced API-related features was not fully verified from a definitive pricing/support statement.

description: Cloud-based customer support and helpdesk software for managing tickets, conversations, and support workflows.
auth_notes: Official Freshdesk API docs indicate API access via a personal API key and also describe OAuth 2.0 app flows; the gathered browser evidence explicitly confirmed personal API key auth, while OAuth support should be human-verified against the current auth docs.
gating_reason: Freshdesk pricing is self-serve and the official API docs note trial accounts have a default API rate limit, indicating API access is available on self-serve trial accounts without sales approval.

api_surface.breadth: Public Freshdesk API v2 documented over HTTPS/JSON with standard CRUD endpoints across core helpdesk resources; breadth appears substantial but was not exhaustively enumerated from gathered evidence.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Freshdesk MCP server or registry entry was verified from gathered evidence. Absence is not definitive and should be human-checked if critical.

blocker: Core REST API appears public and self-serve, but some key fields remain partially unverified from the gathered evidence set alone, especially current OAuth status and authoritative MCP absence.
rate_limit_notes: Gathered evidence indicates plan-based API rate limits, with trial accounts defaulting to 50 calls per minute. Additional per-plan details were not fully verified from the evidence already gathered.
notable_signals: Official developer portal exposes Freshdesk API v2 and rate-limit documentation, suggesting a mature public integration surface. Pricing appears self-serve. Browser-gathered evidence also indicated JSON over HTTPS and standard CRUD semantics.
human_review_reason: The gathered evidence strongly supports public REST API and self-serve access, but some fields remain ambiguous without further verification: whether OAuth 2.0 is currently documented for Freshdesk itself, exhaustive API breadth, and whether an official MCP exists versus simply not being found.

description: Workspace collaboration and CRM platform with a public developer API for app, item, task, and organization data.
auth_notes: Podio developer docs publicly expose API documentation and an auth section, but the exact supported auth flows could not be fully verified from the gathered extracts alone; OAuth2 is strongly indicated and legacy app authentication may also exist, so auth details need human confirmation.
gating_reason: Public developer documentation is available and Podio pricing is publicly self-serve with a free trial path on the pricing page.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer site indicates a broad REST API covering core Podio entities and concepts, with navigation for API reference plus developer forums, partner extensions, and support resources.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No MCP server could be verified from the evidence gathered before stopping; treat as unverified and review manually.

blocker: Authentication specifics, rate-limit policy, and MCP availability were not fully verified from the captured evidence and need manual confirmation before confidently shipping a toolkit.
rate_limit_notes: Rate-limit details were not verified from the gathered evidence.
notable_signals: Official developer portal exists at developers.podio.com. Official pricing page is on Progress domain, suggesting Podio is operated within the Progress product portfolio. Developer portal also references forums, partner extensions, and help/support resources.
human_review_reason: User requested conclusion without further research, but the gathered evidence did not conclusively verify exact auth flows, rate limits, pricing tier implications for API access, or whether an MCP server already exists.

description: Copper is a CRM platform built for Google Workspace teams to manage sales relationships and workflows.
auth_notes: Copper developer docs indicate authentication via API keys and OAuth 2.0.
gating_reason: Copper's pricing page shows 'API & Embedded integration SDK' available on higher paid plans (evidence gathered indicates Professional and Business, not Basic), so API access is not available on the lowest/self-serve free tier.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer documentation shows a RESTful JSON API with getting started, authentication, requests/responses, and resource endpoints for CRM data and bulk APIs.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official MCP server or first-party MCP page was verified from the gathered evidence. This is an absence-of-evidence conclusion and should be human-checked if MCP availability is critical.

blocker: Requires a qualifying paid Copper plan for API access; MCP availability was not verified from first-party evidence.
rate_limit_notes: Gathered evidence indicates API requests are limited to 180 requests per minute, and bulk APIs are limited to 3 requests per second. Exact wording/source section should be human-verified on the requests documentation page.
notable_signals: Official public developer portal exists; docs explicitly mention RESTful JSON API plus both API key and OAuth 2.0 auth. Pricing page includes a named 'API & Embedded integration SDK' feature, indicating productized API access on paid tiers.
human_review_reason: Some details were extracted indirectly through browser-task observations rather than directly quoted in-chat: exact one-line product wording, exact paid-plan mapping for API access, exact rate-limit wording/location, and absence of any MCP page. These fields should be verified manually from the cited first-party pages.

description: Customer communication platform with shared inboxes, collaboration, and support workflow APIs.
auth_notes: Official auth docs state Front uses Bearer token authentication with both OAuth-based integrations and API tokens; MCP docs specifically mention OAuth 2.1 with PKCE.
gating_reason: Official pricing indicates developer tools/Core API are included starting on paid Starter plans; advanced 'connect any messaging platform via API' is limited to Professional and Enterprise.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer platform includes Core API, Analytics API, and Channels API; core API appears broadly usable, while custom channel capabilities are narrower and plan-gated.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Front MCP server is documented as open beta at mcp.frontapp.com/mcp and uses OAuth 2.1 with PKCE.

blocker: Requires a paid Front plan for self-serve API access; some advanced API capabilities are limited to higher tiers.
rate_limit_notes: Official docs list per-minute limits by plan: Starter 50 RPM, Professional 100 RPM, Enterprise 200 RPM, and OAuth partner integrations 120 RPM. Pricing also advertises a Rate Limit Increase add-on for $200/month.
notable_signals: Front has official public developer docs, plan-specific rate limits, a documented MCP server in open beta, and explicit pricing-page signals that core developer tools are included on all paid plans while Channels API-style messaging platform connectivity is more restricted.
human_review_reason: Evidence supports paid self-serve API access and REST docs, but the exact classification of OAuth credentials as JWT-based bearer auth and whether all API surfaces beyond core/analytics are equally accessible across plans is somewhat ambiguous from the gathered pages alone.

description: AI-native B2B support platform across every channel.
auth_notes: Official docs state the API uses Bearer authentication via an API token in the Authorization header; only Admin users can create API tokens in the Pylon dashboard.
gating_reason: Public pricing is listed for paid Starter, Professional, and Enterprise plans; API token creation is restricted to Admin users within a Pylon account.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer docs describe a fairly broad REST API covering Accounts, Activities, Attachments, Audit Logs, Call Recordings, Contacts, Custom Fields, Custom Objects, Feature Requests, Issues, Knowledge Base, Macros, Me, Messages, Surveys, Tags, Tasks & Projects, Teams, Ticket Forms, Training Data, User roles, and Users.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No MCP server or registry entry was verified from the gathered evidence. This remains unconfirmed rather than disproven.

blocker: API access appears buildable from public docs, but token creation requires an Admin user inside a paid Pylon account.
rate_limit_notes: No specific rate limit or throttling documentation was found in the gathered official API, authentication, errors, or API reference pages.
notable_signals: Official public docs say the API supports read/write access and common use cases like fetching customers, fetching issues, and creating issues programmatically. Public pricing is self-serve-visible, and the API reference covers support entities plus admin metadata, suggesting meaningful toolkit surface area.
human_review_reason: MCP availability was not verified from primary evidence, and the exact self-serve API eligibility beyond requiring a paid account plus Admin-created token is somewhat ambiguous from the gathered pages.

description: Customer support platform built for teams and AI agents to work in the same support threads.
auth_notes: Plain GraphQL docs describe authentication via API keys used as Bearer tokens, associated with machine users (`Authorization: Bearer plainApiKey_xxx`).
gating_reason: Plain pricing page shows self-serve Foundation and Horizon plans plus a 7-day free trial; Frontier is custom/contact sales.

api_surface.breadth: Public GraphQL API documented for building on Plain; docs indicate Plain itself is built on the exposed GraphQL API, but breadth of endpoint/domain coverage was not exhaustively verified from the gathered evidence.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official docs mention an MCP Server ('try our MCP Server instead') and agent-focused documentation exists.

rate_limit_notes: No explicit numeric rate limits were verified in gathered evidence. Evidence suggests 429 handling exists via a documented `RateLimitError`, so limits likely exist but are not clearly published in the reviewed pages.
notable_signals: Official GraphQL docs are public; auth is straightforward machine-user API key bearer auth; product explicitly positions itself for AI agents; pricing includes self-serve paid tiers and free trial; official MCP signal present in docs.
human_review_reason: Rate-limit specifics, exact MCP documentation page/implementation details, and full API breadth were not directly verified beyond the gathered pages and browser-extracted notes.

description: Customer support platform with inbox, knowledge base, chat, and embedded support APIs.
auth_notes: Help Scout documents OAuth 2.0 for its Mailbox API, including Authorization Code for third-party apps and Client Credentials for internal integrations; apps are created in My Apps to obtain client credentials.
gating_reason: API access is not available on the Free plan; docs indicate API access on Standard, Plus, and Pro plans, with some endpoints such as Reporting restricted to Plus and Pro.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST APIs include Inbox API 2.0, Docs API, and Webhooks; coverage appears substantial for conversations, customers, mailboxes, knowledge base content, and events, though some endpoint families are plan-restricted.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Help Scout developer and GitHub pages reviewed showed SDKs, app templates, and mobile SDKs, but no verified official MCP server or MCP registry listing was found in the available first-party evidence.

blocker: A toolkit can be built from the documented public REST APIs, but production access requires a paid Help Scout plan because the Free plan excludes API access, and some endpoints are restricted by plan.
rate_limit_notes: Rate limits are account-wide and plan-based: Standard 200 requests/minute, Plus 400/minute, Pro 800/minute. Write requests (POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) count as 2 requests. Docs mention headers including X-RateLimit-Limit-Minute, X-RateLimit-Remaining-Minute, and X-RateLimit-Retry-After.
notable_signals: Help Scout maintains a dedicated developer portal, publishes OAuth and rate-limit docs, exposes multiple API families plus webhooks, and has an official GitHub organization with SDKs and app templates.
human_review_reason: No official MCP server or registry entry was verified from first-party evidence, so MCP existence is marked false based on available evidence rather than an exhaustive ecosystem search.

description: All-in-one customer service platform with AI chatbot, live chat, ticketing, and call center.
auth_notes: Pricing comparison explicitly lists "API keys" by plan, indicating key-based API authentication; browser task could not access the API reference to verify exact header/query usage.
gating_reason: Official pricing evidence indicates API keys are included on paid plans only, with 1 key on Small, 5 on Medium, 10 on Large, and 100 on Enterprise.

api_surface.breadth: Support evidence indicates a complete API reference exists, but the accessible browser session could not open the reference itself, so endpoint breadth is only partially verified.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No MCP server evidence was verified from the gathered sources; this remains uncertain and should be human-reviewed.

blocker: API exists and paid self-serve access appears available, but the official API reference was inaccessible in-browser during research, leaving authentication details and endpoint breadth partially unverified.
rate_limit_notes: No rate-limit documentation was verified from the gathered official pages.
notable_signals: Official support portal surfaced a link labeled "Complete API reference" and a developer changelog link pointing to dev.ladesk.com; official pricing exposes API key allotments by plan, which is a strong signal of maintained API access.
human_review_reason: Key technical fields remain partially unverified because the browser session encountered connection errors opening the API reference/postman pages. REST type and lack of MCP evidence are inferred from support-page labels and absence of verified docs, not fully confirmed from the API documentation itself.

description: Slack is a work operating system for bringing people, projects, apps, and AI together.
auth_notes: Slack developer docs include OAuth installation flows and multiple token types for API access, including bot, user, app-level, workflow, configuration, and service tokens. Evidence gathered indicates token-based bearer auth is central; exact support for every flow variant should be human-verified from the specific auth pages.
gating_reason: Slack provides public developer documentation and self-serve app creation via its developer platform, with no partner-only or contact-sales gate identified in the gathered evidence.

api_surface.breadth: Public Slack developer docs show multiple API families including Web API and other platform APIs/events, indicating broad platform coverage for messaging, workspace apps, and administration; exact scope breadth should be human-verified.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official first-party Slack MCP server or MCP documentation was verified in the gathered evidence.

rate_limit_notes: Slack documents Web API rate limits on an official rate limits page. Detailed tier values and method-specific quotas were not captured in the gathered evidence, so implementers should verify per-method limits directly from the rate-limit docs.
notable_signals: Official Slack developer documentation is public and extensive, with dedicated sections for authentication, APIs, and rate limits. Multiple token classes suggest mature agent/tooling support but also scope-management complexity.
human_review_reason: Access tier, API breadth characterization, MCP absence, and some auth/rate-limit specifics were inferred from partially gathered evidence without a final completed extraction from pricing/access or MCP-specific official pages.

description: Customer service platform with AI and omnichannel support tools for customer experience teams.
auth_notes: Gladly developer docs expose a REST API with an Authentication section and help-center evidence indicates API tokens are generated in Gladly under Settings > App Developer Tools; exact auth header format and whether any additional auth modes exist were not fully verified from the gathered evidence.
gating_reason: Evidence indicates API access is available to existing Gladly customers from the product UI rather than an open public self-serve developer signup; token generation appears to require in-product access and likely an admin/API-user role.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST developer documentation exists, including authentication and rate-limit sections, suggesting a meaningful API surface for Gladly platform operations; breadth beyond those sections was not exhaustively verified from the gathered evidence.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official MCP server evidence was found in the gathered Gladly or GitHub searches; absence is not definitive.

blocker: Requires an existing Gladly customer account and likely admin-level in-product setup to generate API tokens; self-serve access was not verified.
rate_limit_notes: Gathered evidence from the REST rate-limit section indicates a default limit of 10 requests/second for standard REST methods and 10 requests/minute with 2 concurrent requests for the Reports API.
notable_signals: Official developer site exists at developer.gladly.com with REST docs, auth section, and rate-limit section. Help-center evidence suggests API tokens are created from Settings > App Developer Tools, indicating customer-managed token setup inside the product.
human_review_reason: Access tier and auth specifics are inferred from gathered browser evidence rather than directly quoted from a dedicated pricing/access page; MCP absence is also non-definitive without broader verification.

description: Customer support helpdesk with AI agent features for ecommerce brands.
auth_notes: Developer docs indicate private apps authenticate with API keys/access tokens, while public apps use OAuth 2.0. Evidence gathered did not fully clarify whether 'access tokens' should be classified separately from api_key in this schema, so auth classification is normalized to api_key + oauth2.
gating_reason: Private apps appear self-serve, but public app distribution in the Gorgias App Store requires review/approval; because toolkit-style integrations commonly target reusable/public app setups, access gating is at least partially approval-based.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer docs describe a RESTful, resource-oriented JSON API with broad helpdesk coverage including tickets, customers, messages, users, teams, macros, rules, tags, views, statistics, voice calls, widgets, files, jobs, events, and related account resources.

notable_signals: Marketing API is a dedicated ads surface on top of Graph API; auth docs are detailed; access token types include system user tokens useful for automation; security docs recommend appsecret_proof and require TLS 1.2/1.3; access-tier differences strongly affect usable throughput.

gating_reason: Clay pricing indicates native 'Clay API access' is only available on the Enterprise plan, while self-serve tiers expose platform features, webhook automation, and HTTP API integrations for calling external APIs rather than a general public Clay API.

api_surface.breadth: No traditional public general-purpose Clay REST or GraphQL API was verified from the gathered first-party evidence. Official docs instead describe webhook ingestion plus a limited Enterprise-only People and Company API for basic lookups, and official MCP support.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Clay has an official MCP page describing use with ChatGPT, Codex, and Claude.

blocker: No verified self-serve public API for Clay was found; native Clay API access appears Enterprise-gated/contact-sales only, and the public evidence does not document enough auth and endpoint detail to build a general toolkit today.
rate_limit_notes: No public numeric API rate limits were verified in the gathered evidence. The reviewed Clay documentation reportedly notes Clay is not intended for millisecond-response request patterns, but exact limits were not confirmed from visible page text.
notable_signals: Official pricing distinguishes 'HTTP API integrations' and webhook automation from separate Enterprise 'Clay API access'. Official MCP support exists. Official GitHub organization exists, but gathered evidence did not show an active public API reference suitable for immediate toolkit construction.
human_review_reason: Key fields remain ambiguous from the gathered evidence: the exact authentication method for the Enterprise Clay API was not publicly verified, and the 'using-clay-as-an-api' page content was inferred through browser task extraction rather than directly inspectable text in-chat. API surface classification is therefore conservative.

description: Web deployment and hosting platform with APIs for sites, builds, deploys, forms, functions, and account resources.
auth_notes: Netlify documents OAuth2 access tokens for third-party apps and personal access tokens sent as Bearer tokens for direct API use.
gating_reason: Official pricing page shows Free tier support for 'Deploy from AI, Git, or API' and Netlify SDK, indicating self-serve API access without sales approval.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API v1 with endpoints for sites, deploys, builds, forms, functions, environment variables, accounts, teams, and related resources; official docs describe it as Netlify's REST API at api.netlify.com/api/v1.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Netlify has an official MCP server repository, netlify/netlify-mcp, for project management and deployment workflows.

rate_limit_notes: Netlify docs state a general API limit of 500 requests per minute, plus deploy trigger limits of 3 per minute and 100 per day.
notable_signals: Official developer docs are public, auth is documented, free tier includes API-driven deploys, and Netlify publishes an official MCP server on GitHub.

description: Frontend cloud platform whose REST API lets you interact with Vercel projects and deploy applications programmatically.

gating_reason: Evidence gathered from official docs indicates the REST API is publicly documented and usable with account-level access tokens; no contact-sales or admin approval gate was found. Rate-limit docs show plan-based limits (for example Hobby vs Pro), implying self-serve access with higher limits on paid plans rather than API availability being gated.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API docs exist for interacting with Vercel projects and deployments; official docs also expose related integrations and MCP documentation, suggesting a meaningful surface for deployment, project, and platform operations.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Vercel docs include a Vercel MCP server page and the browser run reported it as available on all plans, but the exact wording should be rechecked by a human from the cited docs page.

blocker: Core REST API appears buildable today, but some important implementation details remain insufficiently verified from the captured evidence set, especially exact OAuth/integrations auth documentation and precise access-tier wording for free vs paid usage.
rate_limit_notes: Official limits documentation includes REST API rate limits with plan-based differences (the browser evidence explicitly noted Hobby vs Pro distinctions), but exact numeric thresholds were not reliably extracted into chat and should be human-verified on the limits page before publication.
notable_signals: Official first-party REST API docs are public; authentication uses Bearer access tokens; official integrations docs suggest OAuth support for app integrations; and Vercel publishes official MCP documentation, which is a strong signal for agent/tooling friendliness.
human_review_reason: The existing evidence supports public REST API docs, bearer-token auth, and official MCP existence, but exact OAuth documentation, precise rate-limit values, and definitive self-serve free-tier wording were not fully captured in-chat and should be verified directly from the cited Vercel pages.

api_surface.mcp_notes: Supabase docs include an MCP getting-started page, and official GitHub references were identified for community MCP servers (supabase-mcp and postgres-mcp), but the exact ownership/status should be human-checked.

blocker: Some platform/API auth specifics, especially Management API authentication details, were not fully verified from the captured evidence and should be confirmed manually before shipping a complete toolkit.
rate_limit_notes: A Supabase uptime/rate-limit page was identified as a target, but no limit values were captured from the evidence already gathered; rate-limit specifics need manual verification.
notable_signals: Public first-party docs cover API guides, API key models, API security, pricing, and MCP getting started; GitHub repos suggest MCP ecosystem support around Supabase/Postgres.
human_review_reason: Management API authentication details, exact rate-limit notes, and the precise official/community status of MCP offerings were not fully verified from the captured page evidence.

description: Developer platform and network infrastructure provider with APIs for managing zones, DNS, security, analytics, Workers, and related services.

notable_signals: Official docs explicitly separate REST API and GraphQL Analytics API, publish permission groups spanning many Cloudflare products, and link an official MCP server repository—strong signals of mature agent-facing platform readiness.
human_review_reason: Access-tier classification is inferred from public docs and self-serve token creation guidance rather than a directly cited pricing/access page in the gathered evidence; some product endpoints may require paid Cloudflare plans even though initial API access appears self-serve.

description: Cloud data platform for storing, processing, and analyzing data and building data applications.
auth_notes: Official Snowflake REST API authentication docs list OAuth, key pair authentication via JWT, programmatic access tokens (PAT), and workload identity federation. Snowflake also documents username/password authentication for clients and drivers. Mapping used: OAuth -> oauth2; PAT -> token; username/password -> basic; key pair JWT and workload identity federation -> other.
gating_reason: Snowflake pricing pages show a self-serve 'Start for free' option, alongside contact-sales paths for larger plans.

api_surface.breadth: Documented REST APIs plus SQL API, drivers, and language APIs/SDKs are publicly documented; official docs also list multiple drivers such as Go, JDBC, .NET, Node.js, ODBC, PHP, and Python.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Snowflake MCP server or official MCP documentation was verified from the gathered evidence. This remains uncertain and should be human-reviewed.

rate_limit_notes: No clear official API rate-limit figure was verified from the gathered evidence. Human review needed to confirm whether Snowflake publishes request-rate limits or only service/warehouse concurrency constraints.
notable_signals: Official docs expose a broad developer surface including REST APIs, SQL API, drivers, and language APIs. Authentication docs are detailed and include OAuth, key pair/JWT, PAT, and workload identity federation. Pricing pages indicate both self-serve entry and enterprise sales motions.
human_review_reason: MCP existence was not positively verified from an official source, and rate-limit specifics were not confirmed from the gathered pages. API surface could arguably be classified as Both if Snowflake SQL-over-HTTP is treated separately from REST, but no GraphQL evidence was found.

gating_reason: Neo4j provides publicly accessible documentation, downloadable Community Edition, self-serve Aura signup/pricing, and public docs for multiple application interfaces without partner approval.

The browser run also found official MCP documentation under Neo4j docs, indicating additional agent-oriented integration surface.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Neo4j documentation includes an MCP section at neo4j.com/docs/mcp/current/, which is strong evidence that a Neo4j MCP offering exists.

rate_limit_notes: No explicit API rate-limit policy was verified from the gathered evidence. Neo4j Aura/platform limits may exist in separate docs, but they were not confirmed from terminal output here.
notable_signals: Official docs indicate the legacy HTTP API is deprecated in favor of the Query API. Neo4j also maintains first-party MCP documentation, which is a strong signal for agent/toolkit relevance. Public docs also point developers to Bolt, HTTP/Query API, and GraphQL-oriented application paths.
human_review_reason: Some fields remain partially ambiguous from the gathered evidence only: exact one-line product wording, whether API surface should be classified strictly as REST versus mixed HTTP/protocol plus GraphQL, whether SSO should count as an auth method for toolkit purposes, and whether a first-party documented rate-limit page should change access/buildability nuances.

description: Cloud database platform and control plane for deploying, managing, and monitoring MongoDB clusters and data services.
auth_notes: Official Atlas Administration API docs indicate OAuth 2.0 via service accounts and legacy API keys; the authentication page also references HTTP Digest Authentication for legacy API keys, which is captured under other due to schema constraints.
gating_reason: MongoDB Atlas has public documentation and self-serve access paths, including public pricing/free-tier pages; however, some Atlas Administration API actions require Organization Owner or Project Owner roles.

api_surface.breadth: Public Atlas Administration API with versioned REST endpoints (v1/v2) for Atlas org/project/cluster administration; evidence gathered did not confirm any GraphQL surface for Atlas Administration API.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official MongoDB MCP Server exists via official docs and GitHub; evidence says it supports MongoDB databases and MongoDB Atlas clusters, including Atlas tools.

rate_limit_notes: Official Atlas API rate-limit docs indicate a token bucket algorithm and document default rate limits including a 1200 bucket capacity for certain GROUP scopes and a 500 requests per 60 seconds refill rate; 429 is used when limits are exceeded. Exact per-scope coverage should be human-verified from the rate-limit table.
notable_signals: Official first-party docs cover Atlas Administration API auth, access configuration, and rate limits. Official MongoDB MCP server docs and repository are available, strengthening agent-tooling viability. Docs also note role prerequisites for some API configuration tasks.
human_review_reason: Some details were inferred from browser-captured snippets rather than directly quoted in-chat: exact pricing/free-tier wording, exact legacy auth labeling (API key plus HTTP Digest), and whether Atlas has any separate GraphQL surface outside the gathered Administration API evidence should be human-verified.

description: Developer-first application monitoring platform with a public web API for managing account resources and exporting data.
auth_notes: Official API auth docs show bearer-token authentication via user auth tokens and internal integration tokens, and also document OAuth2 support for integrations; token scoping differs between user tokens and integration tokens.
gating_reason: Official pricing page includes a $0 Developer self-serve plan, and gathered evidence indicates API access is included on the Developer plan.

api_surface.mcp_notes: Sentry has an official MCP offering, with docs at mcp.sentry.dev and official getsentry GitHub repositories including sentry-mcp; gathered evidence indicates beta status.

rate_limit_notes: Official rate-limit docs describe fixed-window limits and response headers including X-Sentry-Rate-Limit-Limit, X-Sentry-Rate-Limit-Remaining, and X-Sentry-Rate-Limit-Reset.

Access-tier conclusion is evidence-backed but should be spot-checked by a human on the live pricing page.

description: Workspace platform for docs, databases, and collaboration with a public developer API for Notion content and integrations.

api_surface.mcp_notes: Official Notion developer docs include a hosted Notion MCP server overview and setup guidance.

description: Observability and security platform with APIs for monitoring, logs, traces, incidents, security, and related operations.

gating_reason: Datadog has self-serve signup and a free tier/trial path on its pricing page rather than partner or admin gating.

api_surface.mcp_notes: Datadog has an official MCP server product/docs presence, but the exact implementation/repository details were not fully verified from the gathered evidence.

description: Airtable provides a Web API for interacting with Airtable bases, tables, views, and records.

api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Airtable MCP server was verified from the gathered evidence. Browser research attempted MCP verification but did not reach a confirmed first-party MCP source, so this is treated as unverified and needs human review.

rate_limit_notes: Official rate limits page indicates 5 requests/second per base and 50 requests/second per user or service account token; exceeding limits triggers temporary throttling and clients should use backoff/retry behavior.
notable_signals: Official docs show active API maintenance, including authentication migration away from legacy API keys and a changelog page. Billing plans page indicates many endpoints are available on self-serve plans, while certain enterprise/org capabilities are gated to Enterprise Scale.
human_review_reason: MCP existence/absence was not conclusively verified from a first-party or authoritative registry source in the gathered evidence. Plan-based endpoint breadth may also need a quick human pass if downstream requires precise endpoint-by-plan mapping.

description: Linear provides a public GraphQL API and SDKs for building apps and integrations for Linear.
auth_notes: Official developer docs mention OAuth 2.0 authentication and personal API keys. Verified header examples include `Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>` for OAuth and `Authorization: <KEY>` for personal API keys; personal API key classification is marked as token-based.
gating_reason: Developer documentation is publicly accessible and the gathered evidence shows standard OAuth 2.0 and personal API key auth with no verified contact-sales or approval gate for initial API use.

api_surface.breadth: Public API is GraphQL and described as the same API Linear uses internally. Gathered schema evidence indicates broad coverage with roughly 161 queries, 370 mutations, 79 subscriptions, and 567 object types, covering core work-management entities such as issues, projects, cycles, teams, and documents.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official MCP server or official MCP documentation was verified from the gathered evidence. This is treated as unverified absence, so human review is recommended.

rate_limit_notes: Official rate limiting page states Linear uses a leaky bucket algorithm. Gathered evidence indicates hourly request limits of 2,500/hour for API keys and 5,000/hour for OAuth apps, plus complexity-based limits including a 3,000,000/hour complexity limit for API keys and a 10,000 maximum complexity per query. Rate-limit headers are documented, including request and complexity limit/remaining/reset headers.
notable_signals: Official developer hub exists; public GraphQL docs are available; Apollo-hosted public schema reference is exposed; docs state the public API is the same one used internally; documented OAuth and personal API key auth support straightforward agent integration.
human_review_reason: Self-serve access was inferred from the public docs and standard auth setup but not directly confirmed on a pricing/access page in the gathered evidence. MCP existence was not verified from an official source; only lack of evidence was observed.

api_surface.breadth: Documented REST API under /api/v2 with endpoints for transcript extraction, video info, YouTube search, channel videos, and playlist videos; MCP offering exposes related tools for LLM clients.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Official docs include an MCP section, and the gathered evidence identifies an official GitHub MCP server at ZeroPointRepo/youtube-mcp with Smithery and Glama listings.

rate_limit_notes: Gathered evidence indicates 200 RPM on the free tier and 300 RPM on paid tiers; docs also note credits are consumed only on successful 200 OK requests.
notable_signals: Official docs publish base URL, endpoint set, credit costs, and MCP auth guidance. Homepage pricing appears public, while billing management is login-gated. No dedicated public changelog was found; updates may instead be reflected through the GitHub repo history.
human_review_reason: Some findings rely on browser-run extraction summaries rather than directly quoted page text in-chat, especially pricing/rate-limit details and official status of the GitHub MCP repo; these are plausible from gathered evidence but should be spot-checked by a human.
